explanation_en: 'Morgen' (with capital M) means 'morning'. It is a masculine noun. Note: 'morgen' (lowercase) means 'tomorrow'.

question.de: _____ Morgen beginnt sehr früh für uns.
question.en: The morning starts very early for us.

explanation_en: 'Morgen' (morning) is masculine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'der'.

question.en: The morning is my favourite time of day.

explanation_en: 'Morgen' means 'morning'. It is the masculine subject, used with 'der'.

question.de: Am _____ trinke ich immer eine Tasse Kaffee.
question.en: In the morning I always drink a cup of coffee.

explanation_en: 'Am Morgen' = 'in the morning'. 'am' = an + dem (dative). Masculine dative: 'dem Morgen' → 'am Morgen'.
'Abend' means 'evening'. It is a masculine noun taking the article 'der'.

question.en: The evening was really lovely.

explanation_en: 'Abend' is masculine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'der'.

question.en: The evening starts after work.

explanation_en: 'Abend' means 'evening'. It is the masculine subject, used with 'der'.

question.en: In the evening we often go for a walk.

explanation_en: 'Am Abend' = 'in the evening'. 'am' = an + dem (dative masculine). The noun itself is unchanged.
'Nacht' means 'night'. It is a feminine noun taking the article 'die'.

question.en: The night was very loud.

explanation_en: 'Nacht' is feminine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'die'.

question.de: Die _____ ist sehr dunkel und ruhig.
question.en: The night is very dark and quiet.

explanation_en: 'Nacht' means 'night'. It is the feminine subject, used with 'die'.

question.de: In der _____ kann ich nicht schlafen.
question.en: At night I cannot sleep.

explanation_en: 'In der Nacht' = 'at night'. 'in der' is feminine dative. The noun 'Nacht' is unchanged.
'Brot' means 'bread'. It is a neuter noun taking the article 'das'.

question.en: The bread is still fresh.

explanation_en: 'Brot' is neuter. As the nominative subject, it takes 'das'.

question.de: Das _____ kommt frisch aus der Bäckerei.
question.en: The bread comes fresh from the bakery.

explanation_en: 'Brot' means 'bread'. It is the neuter subject, used with 'das'.

question.de: Ich kaufe jeden Morgen ein frisches _____.
question.en: I buy a fresh loaf of bread every morning.

explanation_en: After 'ein frisches' (neuter accusative adjective), 'Brot' is unchanged — neuter nominative and accusative forms are identical.
'Milch' means 'milk'. It is a feminine noun taking the article 'die'. It has no common plural form.

question.en: The milk expired today.

explanation_en: 'Milch' is feminine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'die'.

question.en: The milk in the fridge is cold.

explanation_en: 'Milch' means 'milk'. It is the feminine subject, used with 'die'.

question.en: Can you please buy the milk?

explanation_en: 'kaufen' takes the accusative. Feminine nouns look the same in nominative and accusative: 'die Milch'.
'Kaffee' means 'coffee'. It is a masculine noun taking the article 'der'.

question.en: The coffee is too hot.

explanation_en: 'Kaffee' is masculine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'der'.

question.en: The coffee smells wonderful.

explanation_en: 'Kaffee' means 'coffee'. It is the masculine subject, used with 'der'.

question.de: Ich trinke jeden Morgen meinen _____ ohne Zucker.
question.en: I drink my coffee without sugar every morning.

explanation_en: 'meinen Kaffee' — masculine accusative uses 'meinen'. The noun 'Kaffee' is unchanged.
'Sonne' means 'sun'. It is a feminine noun taking the article 'die'.

question.en: The sun is shining today.

explanation_en: 'Sonne' is feminine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'die'.

question.en: The sun rises in the east.

explanation_en: 'Sonne' means 'sun'. It is the feminine subject, used with 'die'.

question.de: Wir genießen die warme _____ am Strand.
question.en: We enjoy the warm sun on the beach.

explanation_en: 'genießen' takes the accusative. Feminine nouns look the same in nominative and accusative: 'die warme Sonne'.
'Regen' means 'rain'. It is a masculine noun taking the article 'der'.

question.en: The rain doesn't stop.

explanation_en: 'Regen' is masculine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'der'.

question.en: The rain is very heavy today.

explanation_en: 'Regen' means 'rain'. It is the masculine subject, used with 'der'.

question.de: Wegen des starken _____ bleiben wir zu Hause.
question.en: Because of the heavy rain we are staying at home.

explanation_en: 'Wegen' takes the genitive case. Masculine genitive: 'des Regens' (adding -s to the noun).
'Berg' means 'mountain'. It is a masculine noun taking the article 'der'.

question.en: The mountain is over 3000 metres high.

explanation_en: 'Berg' is masculine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'der'.

question.de: Der _____ ist im Winter mit Schnee bedeckt.
question.en: The mountain is covered with snow in winter.

explanation_en: 'Berg' means 'mountain'. It is the masculine subject, used with 'der'.

question.de: Wir wandern auf den _____ und genießen die Aussicht.
question.en: We hike up the mountain and enjoy the view.

explanation_en: 'auf den Berg' — movement upward uses 'auf' + accusative. Masculine accusative: 'auf den Berg'.
'Fluss' means 'river'. It is a masculine noun taking the article 'der'.

question.en: The river is very wide.

explanation_en: 'Fluss' is masculine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'der'.

question.en: The river flows through the city.

explanation_en: 'Fluss' means 'river'. It is the masculine subject, used with 'der'.

question.de: Wir überqueren den _____ mit dem Boot.
question.en: We cross the river by boat.

explanation_en: 'überqueren' takes the accusative. 'den Fluss' — masculine accusative uses 'den'.
'Problem' means 'problem'. It is a neuter noun taking the article 'das'.

question.en: The problem is solved.

explanation_en: 'Problem' is neuter. As the nominative subject, it takes 'das'.

question.de: Das _____ ist nicht leicht zu lösen.
question.en: The problem is not easy to solve.

explanation_en: 'Problem' means 'problem'. It is the neuter subject, used with 'das'.

question.de: Ich habe ein großes _____ mit meinem Auto.
question.en: I have a big problem with my car.

explanation_en: After 'ein großes' (neuter accusative adjective), 'Problem' is unchanged — neuter accusative is identical to nominative.
'Idee' means 'idea'. It is a feminine noun taking the article 'die'.

question.en: The idea is very creative.

explanation_en: 'Idee' is feminine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'die'.

question.en: The idea is really good.

explanation_en: 'Idee' means 'idea'. It is the feminine subject, used with 'die'.

question.de: Ich habe eine gute _____ für das Projekt.
question.en: I have a good idea for the project.

explanation_en: After 'eine gute' (feminine indefinite article + adjective in accusative), 'Idee' is unchanged — feminine accusative is the same form as nominative.
'Meinung' means 'opinion'. It is a feminine noun taking the article 'die'.

question.en: The teacher's opinion is important.

explanation_en: 'Meinung' is feminine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'die'.

question.de: Meine _____ ist, dass wir mehr Pausen brauchen.
question.en: My opinion is that we need more breaks.

explanation_en: 'Meinung' means 'opinion'. After 'meine' (feminine nominative), the noun is unchanged.

question.de: Ich respektiere deine _____, auch wenn ich anderer Ansicht bin.
question.en: I respect your opinion, even if I see it differently.

explanation_en: After 'deine' (possessive, feminine accusative), 'Meinung' is unchanged — feminine nominative and accusative are identical.
'Grund' means 'reason' or 'ground'. It is a masculine noun taking the article 'der'.

question.de: _____ Grund für seinen Fehler ist unklar.
question.en: The reason for his mistake is unclear.

explanation_en: 'Grund' is masculine. As the nominative subject, it takes 'der'.

question.de: Der _____ für ihre Entscheidung ist verständlich.
question.en: The reason for her decision is understandable.

explanation_en: 'Grund' means 'reason'. It is the masculine subject, used with 'der'.

question.en: There is no reason to worry.

explanation_en: After 'keinen' (masculine accusative of 'kein'), 'Grund' is unchanged — masculine accusative uses 'keinen' before the unchanged noun.
